title: Pierre Robin Sequence: Airway Management
objectives: Understand the sequence of events in Pierre Robin
Apply management algorithm from conservative to surgical

sections.title: Pathophysiology
sections.content:
**The Sequence:**

1. **Micrognathia** (primary defect)
   ↓
2. **Glossoptosis** (tongue falls back)
   ↓
3. **Airway Obstruction**
   ↓
4. **U-shaped Cleft Palate** (tongue prevents palate fusion)

**Key Concept:** This is a SEQUENCE (one defect leads to others), not a syndrome

**Incidence:** 1/8,500-14,000 births

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Airway Obstruction:**
• Worse when supine
• Improves with prone positioning
• Stertor, stridor
• Retractions, desaturations
• Cyanotic episodes

**Feeding Difficulties:**
• Poor coordination
• Aspiration risk
• Failure to thrive

**Physical Exam:**
• Retrognathic mandible
• Tongue position against posterior pharynx
• Wide U-shaped cleft palate

sections.title: Airway Assessment
sections.content:
**Severity Classification:**

**Mild:**
• Obstruction only in supine position
• Relieved by prone positioning
• No desaturations

**Moderate:**
• Obstruction in multiple positions
• Intermittent desaturations
• Feeding difficulties

**Severe:**
• Obstruction despite positioning
• Frequent/prolonged desaturations
• Unable to feed orally
• May require intubation

**Workup:**
• Sleep study (polysomnography)
• Flexible laryngoscopy
• Swallow study if feeding issues

sections.title: Management - Conservative
sections.content:
**Positioning:**
• Prone or lateral positioning
• Effective in 50-70% of cases
• Gravity helps tongue fall forward

**Nasopharyngeal Airway:**
• Bypasses tongue obstruction
• Bridge to definitive treatment
• Requires monitoring

**Feeding Support:**
• Special nipples/bottles
• NG tube if inadequate oral intake
• Upright positioning during feeds
• May need G-tube

**Non-invasive Ventilation:**
• CPAP for moderate cases
• Stents airway open

sections.title: Management - Surgical
sections.content:
**Indications for Surgery:**
• Failed conservative management
• Severe obstruction
• Failure to thrive
• Recurrent aspiration

**Surgical Options:**

**Tongue-Lip Adhesion:**
• Temporary procedure
• Sutures tongue to lip
• Prevents glossoptosis

**Mandibular Distraction Osteogenesis:**
• Gradually lengthens mandible
• Addresses underlying micrognathia
• 1mm/day advancement
• Most definitive treatment

**Tracheostomy:**
• Last resort for severe cases
• Bypasses upper airway entirely
• Significant morbidity

sections.title: Associated Syndromes
sections.content:
**Isolated PRS:** 40-50%

**Syndromic PRS:** 50-60%

**Stickler Syndrome (most common):**
• Myopia, retinal detachment
• Joint hypermobility
• Collagen disorder

**Velocardiofacial/22q11:**
• Cardiac defects
• Immune deficiency

**Treacher Collins:**
• Mandibulofacial dysostosis

**Fetal Alcohol Syndrome**

**Evaluation:**
• Genetics consultation
• Echocardiogram
• Ophthalmology exam
• Hearing evaluation

keyTakeaways: Pierre Robin is a SEQUENCE, not a syndrome
Micrognathia is the primary defect leading to other features
50-60% of cases are associated with syndromes (especially Stickler)
Mandibular distraction addresses the underlying cause

reviewQuestions.question: A newborn with micrognathia and cleft palate has oxygen desaturations while supine that resolve when placed prone. Polysomnography shows AHI of 8. What is the most appropriate initial management?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Mild-moderate Pierre Robin sequence often responds to conservative positioning. Prone positioning allows gravity to pull the tongue forward, relieving obstruction. Surgery is reserved for failed conservative management.
